meta.setting: Mining claim stakes, paper notices, witness cuts, survey chains, and contested hillsides around French Gulch
meta.rdr_clone_target: RDR-clone western with land disputes, claim jumping, law papers, and armed intimidation

research_grounding.title: NPS Whiskeytown History and Culture
research_grounding.use: Mining claims, settlement pressure, and changing land use in Whiskeytown country.

research_grounding.title: California State Parks Shasta State Historic Park
research_grounding.use: Gold-rush legal and town context for claim-era disputes.

generation_prompt.task: Generate the Claim Stakes region page for the 1899 Jefferson State western packet. This JSON contains shape and guidance only.
page_shape: H3 local marker line in the register of Split Cedar, Tin Notice, and Witness Cut
square image reference immediately after the marker line
opening blockquote built as three short generated lines: boundary object, weather or animal damage, plain table meaning
two compact paragraphs establishing claim stakes as legal tool, provocation, evidence trail, route marker, and scene fuse
closing blockquote that gives a generated claim-stake sign and practical table meaning
generation_guidance: Generate claim stakes as fragile law in the dirt: split cedar posts, tacked notices, survey chain scars, cut brush, boot tracks, and someone watching from the trees.
Give the table hooks around claim jumping, forged notices, old Native use paths crossing new boundaries, displaced grazing, missing witnesses, and a paper nailed upside down after rain.
Let stakes be small objects with large consequences; the page should make boundaries feel uncertain, personal, and dangerous.
Use legal dust, not courtroom polish: county seal wax, tin tobacco boxes used for papers, pencil marks, damp signatures, and hard looks.
generation_prompt.hand_cut_note: A claim stake is just a stick until a man brings a rifle to read it.
generation_prompt.table_spark: A claim-stake scene should ask who drove the stake, who moved it, who saw it first, and who cannot afford the answer.
must_preserve: Use 1899 western mining-claim and road language only.
The generated page must include image path regions/images/claim-stakes.jpg.
The prompt must not contain final prose, final blockquotes, or finished field marks.
avoid: do not mention any prior packet, inherited taxonomy, or non-western source language
do not turn boundary signs into sacred objects or invented ceremonial markings
do not use modern survey gear, printed barcodes, laminated notices, or GPS language
self_check: The future markdown follows the exact heading sequence.
The future page is about claim boundaries as conflict tools and scene engines.
The prompt contains no finished page prose.
All language belongs to grounded 1899 Jefferson State western play.
